teachingHint: Your child is learning the /th/ sound. When 't' and 'h' sit together, they make one sound: /th/. There are two versions — a quiet /th/ (as in 'thin', tongue between teeth, no voice) and a buzzy /th/ (as in 'this', tongue between teeth, with voice). Help your child spot 'th' and blend: th-i-n → thin. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: th-i-n → The letters 'th' make one sound /th/. Blend: th-i-n → thin

th-u-d → The letters 'th' make one sound /th/. Blend: th-u-d → thud

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! The 'e' says its name 'ee'. We say 'hee'. Learn it by sight.

story: A thin cat jumped on a thick log — thud! He was so thin he slid off the thick log, and he landed with a big thud!

story: We ran down the path to the bath, but a duck was in the bath doing math! We went back up the path and the duck said the math was too hard for a bath.

story: This dog chased that cat — it was so funny to see them run! This dog bumped into that cat, and them two fell in a heap — it was a big mess!

story: A frog is bigger than a bug — then the bug sat with me and said sit with me! The frog was bigger than the bug, but then they were happy to sit with each other.

teachingHint: Your child is learning the /ng/ sound. When 'n' and 'g' sit together at the end of a word, they make one sound: /ng/ (like the end of 'sing'). It's not /n/ then /g/ — it's one sound made at the back of the throat. Help your child spot 'ng' and blend: r-i-ng → ring. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

story: The king tied a string to his wing — my string, on my wing! The silly king flew up with the string on his wing, and they all cheered.
